title: Community Health Worker (CHW) Sustainability and allied professions in Arizona
authors_full: Alicia Hernandez¹,², Binoli Herath¹, Mindy McEntee, PhD¹
authors_short: Alicia Hernandez Binoli Herath Mindy McEntee

summary.hook: This poster reports preliminary findings from structured qualitative interviews with trainees in ASU’s Community Health Worker (CHW) Training Program who previously worked as...
summary.summary_30s: This poster reports preliminary findings from structured qualitative interviews with trainees in ASU’s Community Health Worker (CHW) Training Program who previously worked as Behavioral Health Technicians (BHTs), peer recovery support specialists (PRSS), or other behavioral health paraprofessionals.

audience_versions.general: This poster reports preliminary findings from structured qualitative interviews with trainees in ASU’s Community Health Worker (CHW) Training Program who previously worked as Behavioral Health Technicians (BHTs), peer recovery support specialists (PRSS), or other behavioral health paraprofessionals.

audience_versions.clinician: This poster reports preliminary findings from structured qualitative interviews with trainees in ASU’s Community Health Worker (CHW) Training Program who previously... Data collection ongoing; 9 participants interviewed to date. For clinicians, the main value is whether the findings could inform care decisions, screening, workflow, or patient communication.
audience_versions.community: This poster reports preliminary findings from structured qualitative interviews with trainees in ASU’s Community Health Worker (CHW) Training Program who previously... Clarifying boundaries between CHWs, BHTs, and PRSS could reduce role confusion and improve team integration. For community partners, the key question is who benefits, who might be left out, and how the work could translate into real-world impact.

talktrack_2m.what_they_did: Qualitative study (structured interviews \+ thematic analysis).. * n=9 interviews completed to date (data collection ongoing)

key_numbers: n=9 interviews completed to date (data collection ongoing).
Nine participants interviewed to date; ages 25–62; 7 females.
Participants identified as White (2), Hispanic (4), Black (2), Other (1).
Frequent themes include organizational role hierarchy (7 mentions) and workforce sustainability/compensation (6...
Compensation perceived as inadequate; suggested wage range $20–$25/hour.
takeaways: Data collection ongoing; 9 participants interviewed to date.
Poster includes a “theme” table summarizing categories and participant count per theme (exact counts are readable for each row) and a note: “4 out of 9 interviewees...

general: What is the main takeaway you hope attendees remember from this poster?
What surprised you most once you started digging into this topic?
Why does this work matter beyond this specific poster session?
undergrad: What part of this project would be most helpful for an undergraduate student to learn from?
What skills or tools were most important for getting this project done?
If an undergraduate wanted to get involved in work like this, where would you suggest they start?
grad: How did you identify and recruit trainees with prior BHT/PRSS experience—was it purposive sampling from applications?
What was the key design or analysis tradeoff in this project?
If you were extending this into a next study, what would you tighten or measure differently?
researcher: How did you identify and recruit trainees with prior BHT/PRSS experience—was it purposive sampling from applications?
What were the key sections of your interview guide (role responsibilities, boundaries, training, compensation, recognition), and how did you probe comparisons across roles?
How did you define CHW vs BHT vs PRSS for the purpose of analysis—did you use formal scope-of-practice descriptions?
clinician: How could these findings affect clinical care, screening, or decision-making in practice?
What kind of patient or care setting would this poster be most relevant for?
What would need to happen before this work would be ready for broader clinical use?
community: Who in the community is most likely to benefit if this work moves forward?
What kinds of community partnerships would strengthen the next phase of this project?
What would meaningful real-world impact look like for the people touched by this work?
leader: What type of support, infrastructure, or investment would help this work move further?
What is the clearest implementation or scale-up opportunity that comes out of this poster?
If a leader wanted to back the next step, what outcome would be most important to track?
